topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a sound that seemed to amplify the frantic thumping in Elias’s chest. He stared at the application form, the crisp white paper mocking him with its blank spaces. He’d spent three sleepless nights polishing his resume, convinced that any of it would somehow translate into merit. When he finally looked up, he saw Maya across the small table, a bright, easy smile playing on her lips as she scrolled through her own documents. A cold knot tightened in his stomach. He felt like a poorly constructed piece of furniture, destined to be overlooked. He picked up his pen, his hand trembling so badly the ink threatened to bleed. *This isn't going to work,* a small, defeated voice whispered in the hollow space behind his ribs.

[story 2]

"Are you sure about this, Alex?" Chloe asked, leaning back in her chair, her tone laced with genuine concern. Alex just shrugged, the movement stiff and unconvincing. He felt a heavy cloak settling around his shoulders, suffocating him. He’d poured everything into this opportunity, yet it felt like trying to push a boulder uphill. When he glanced at Chloe, her confident posture seemed to radiate an effortless capability that he simply didn't possess. He fiddled with the edge of his sleeve, his gaze dropping to the scuffed floor tiles. Every nerve ending screamed that he was an imposter, a fraud about to be exposed. The sheer weight of expectation pressed down, making his breath catch in his throat.

[story 3]

I reread the job description for the third time, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. My palms were slick against the glossy paper. Beside me, Sarah was humming softly, completely absorbed in her own submission. I watched her, noting the relaxed set of her shoulders, the way she casually tapped her pen against her notebook. A profound sense of inadequacy washed over me, a deep, internal chill that had nothing to do with the room temperature. I felt physically heavy, anchored to the spot, as if my very presence was an imposition. When I finally signed my name, the motion felt clumsy, inadequate, a small, insignificant gesture in a world clearly designed for others.
